meta.title: Full-Stack Mastery: Top 0.001% Developer Guide
meta.author: Principal Engineer / CTO Perspective
meta.audience: Serious engineers committed to elite-level mastery
meta.disclaimer: This guide assumes long-term commitment. No shortcuts. No shortcuts will work.

elite_expectations.title: What Top Companies Actually Expect
elite_expectations.subtitle: The unfiltered truth from people who hire and fire at FAANG+ level

core_mindset.systems_thinking: Senior engineers don't solve problems—they design systems that prevent problems. You must instinctively think in terms of scale, failure modes, and second-order consequences. Before writing a line of code, you should be able to describe what happens at 10x, 100x, and 1000x current load.
core_mindset.ownership_mentality: You are responsible for the full lifecycle: design, implementation, deployment, monitoring, incident response, and deprecation. 'It works on my machine' is a junior engineer phrase. Senior engineers own production.
core_mindset.decision_authority: Elite engineers make irreversible decisions confidently and reversible decisions quickly. You must be comfortable with ambiguity, make calls without perfect information, and defend your decisions under pressure.
core_mindset.technical_taste: Beyond skills, top engineers have taste—an intuition for what is elegant vs. overengineered, what will age well vs. become debt. This comes from reading great code, post-mortems, and architecture papers.

technical_depth_vs_breadth.principle: T-shaped or π-shaped knowledge. Deep expertise in 1-2 areas, functional competence across the stack.
technical_depth_vs_breadth.senior_balance: A senior engineer is an expert in their domain but never says 'that's not my job.' They can debug a production database query, review a frontend bundle, and challenge an infrastructure decision—all in the same day.
anti_patterns: The generalist who can't go deep on anything under pressure
The specialist who can't communicate across domains
The engineer who mistakes familiarity with mastery

leadership_qualities.code_review_standard: Your reviews teach, not just gatekeep. You explain the why, link to documentation, and model the standard. Engineers should feel better after your review, not defensive.
leadership_qualities.mentoring: The best senior engineers make the people around them faster. Knowledge hoarding is a red flag. Your impact is measured by your team's output, not your personal commits.
leadership_qualities.architecture_influence: You don't wait to be asked—you propose, document ADRs (Architecture Decision Records), and drive alignment. You influence decisions you don't own.
leadership_qualities.cross_team_collaboration: You are a force multiplier. You know how to work with product, design, data, and ops. You translate technical constraints into business language and business requirements into engineering constraints.

code_quality_markers.production_readiness: Code that isn't observable, recoverable, and deployable without you present is not production-ready. Metrics, logging, feature flags, rollback plans—these are not optional extras.
code_quality_markers.scalability_consciousness: Top engineers write for scale by default—not premature optimization, but designing data models, APIs, and system boundaries that don't require rewrites when success arrives.
code_quality_markers.performance_consciousness: You know the cost of every abstraction. You can reason about memory, CPU, I/O, and network tradeoffs. You've profiled real production systems.
code_quality_markers.security_by_design: Security is not a checklist—it's a mindset baked into every design decision. You think like an attacker.

evaluation_metrics.impact: Shipped features with measurable business outcomes. Incidents prevented. Systems that outlast your tenure.
evaluation_metrics.velocity_multiplier: How fast does your team move because of you? This is the senior+ metric.
evaluation_metrics.scope_expansion: Junior: task. Mid: project. Senior: system. Staff: org. Principal: company.
evaluation_metrics.technical_reputation: Other senior engineers seek your review. PMs want you in design reviews. Your opinions shape direction.

resources.title: The Staff Engineer's Path
resources.why: Definitive guide on technical leadership without people management

resources.title: Stripe Engineering Blog
resources.why: World-class writing on production-grade engineering decisions

resources.title: Netflix Tech Blog
resources.why: Real-world distributed systems challenges at scale

resources.title: Charity Majors on Engineering Levels
resources.why: Brutally honest perspective on what senior actually means

topics: SEO fundamentals: meta, structured data, OpenGraph
Forms: constraint validation API, progressive enhancement
html.mastery_marker: You can build a fully accessible, SEO-optimized, progressively enhanced page without a framework.

topics: CSS architecture: BEM, utility-first, CSS-in-JS tradeoffs
Layout mastery: Flexbox, Grid, Container Queries
CSS Custom Properties and design tokens
css.mastery_marker: You can implement any design with pixel-perfect fidelity and explain every browser quirk encountered.

topics: Event loop, call stack, microtask/macrotask queues
TypeScript advanced patterns: generics, conditional types, decorators
Module system: ESM, CommonJS, bundler implications
Web APIs: Intersection Observer, ResizeObserver, Web Workers
javascript.mastery_marker: You can debug any JavaScript behavior from first principles without Stack Overflow.

react_core: React 19 features: Server Components, use() hook
Performance: memo, useMemo, useCallback—when and why NOT to use them
nextjs: App Router vs Pages Router—architectural tradeoffs
Rendering strategies: SSR, SSG, ISR, PPR (Partial Prerendering)
Caching layers: Request Memoization, Data Cache, Full Route Cache
Middleware: edge computing, A/B testing, auth
react_nextjs.mastery_marker: You can architect a Next.js app that handles millions of users, explain every caching decision, and debug hydration mismatches.

decision_matrix.server_state: TanStack Query or SWR. Not Redux. Server state ≠ UI state.
decision_matrix.ui_state: useState + Context for simple. Zustand for complex UI state. Redux Toolkit only for large teams with complex update logic.
decision_matrix.url_state: nuqs or URLSearchParams. Massively underused. Bookmarkable, shareable state for free.
decision_matrix.form_state: React Hook Form + Zod. Never roll your own form state management.

anti_patterns: Global state for server data (the original Redux sin)
Over-engineering simple toggle state into a state machine
Context causing full re-renders instead of atomic state

performance.core_web_vitals: LCP, INP, CLS—you must be able to diagnose and fix all three in production
techniques: Bundle analysis with webpack-bundle-analyzer or Vite rollup visualizer
Image optimization: modern formats, responsive images, lazy loading
Third-party script management: defer, async, partytown
Virtualization for large lists: TanStack Virtual
tools: Web Vitals monitoring: real user data via SpeedCurve or Vercel Analytics

frontend_security.xss: Content Security Policy, output encoding, DOMPurify for user-generated content
frontend_security.csrf: SameSite cookies, CSRF tokens, understanding when each matters
frontend_security.auth: HttpOnly cookies vs. localStorage—always HttpOnly for tokens

pyramid.unit: Pure functions, custom hooks (React Testing Library + userEvent). Fast, isolated. 70% of tests.
pyramid.integration: Component trees, API mock layers (MSW). 20% of tests.
pyramid.e2e: Critical user journeys only (Playwright). 10% of tests.
pyramid.visual: Storybook + Chromatic for design system components

testing.philosophy: Test behavior, not implementation. If your test breaks when you rename a variable, it's a bad test.

resources.title: JavaScript.info
resources.why: Best comprehensive JavaScript resource, covers deep internals

resources.title: web.dev Performance
resources.why: Google's official guide to Core Web Vitals and performance

resources.title: Josh Comeau's CSS for JavaScript Developers
resources.why: The best CSS course for engineers who want to truly understand it

resources.title: TkDodo's Blog on React Query
resources.why: The most insightful writing on React Query and practical React patterns

resources.title: Next.js Docs
resources.why: The primary source—read it end to end

rest.when: Default choice for most CRUD APIs. Understood universally. HTTP caching works naturally.
mastery: HTTP semantics: idempotency, safety, status codes
HATEOAS (know it, don't always use it)

graphql.when: Multiple clients with different data needs. Reduces over-fetching. Higher complexity cost.

grpc.when: Internal service-to-service. High throughput. Strongly typed contracts. Not for public APIs.

authentication.jwt: Stateless but unrevokable. Short expiry (15min) + refresh token pattern. Store tokens in HttpOnly cookies.
authentication.sessions: Stateful but revokable. Better for security-sensitive apps. Use Redis for distributed sessions.
authentication.oauth2_oidc: For third-party auth. Understand flows: Authorization Code + PKCE (always), not Implicit.
authentication.passkeys: The future. WebAuthn. Start learning now.

authorization.rbac: Role-Based Access Control. Simple and understood. Good starting point.
authorization.abac: Attribute-Based Access Control. More flexible, more complex. Use when RBAC creates too many roles.
authorization.casbin: Policy engine for complex permission systems
authorization.opa: Open Policy Agent for infrastructure-level policies

indexing: B-tree vs. Hash vs. GiST vs. GIN—know when each applies
Composite index column ordering (equality then range)
Index bloat and VACUUM in PostgreSQL
EXPLAIN ANALYZE—you must be able to read query plans
advanced: Read replicas and replication lag considerations
sql_mastery.acid: Understand isolation levels: READ UNCOMMITTED, READ COMMITTED, REPEATABLE READ, SERIALIZABLE. Know which problems each prevents (dirty reads, non-repeatable reads, phantom reads).

nosql.document: MongoDB/Firestore: flexible schema, denormalized data. Good for hierarchical data. Bad for complex joins.
nosql.key_value: Redis/DynamoDB: O(1) access. Use for caching, sessions, rate limiting, leaderboards.
nosql.column_family: Cassandra/DynamoDB: write-optimized, partition-based. Design tables around query patterns first.
nosql.graph: Neo4j/Amazon Neptune: relationship-heavy data. Social graphs, recommendation engines.
nosql.time_series: InfluxDB/TimescaleDB: metrics, IoT data. Built-in time-based aggregations.

databases.data_modeling: The most undervalued skill. Bad data models cause years of pain. Normalize for integrity, denormalize for performance, and know why you're doing either.

layers.cdn: Cloudflare/CloudFront: static assets and edge caching. Furthest from the server, most impactful.
layers.application: Redis/Memcached: computed results, sessions, rate limiting. Cache-aside vs. write-through vs. write-behind.
layers.database: Query result cache. Avoid if possible—hard to invalidate correctly.
layers.in_memory: Process-level cache. Fastest but not distributed. Use for static config, hot paths.

caching.cache_invalidation: The hardest problem in CS (after naming). Strategies: TTL, event-driven invalidation (cache tags), version-based keys. Never cache what you can't invalidate.

queues: BullMQ (Redis-backed, Node.js): excellent for most use cases
RabbitMQ: when you need advanced routing, dead letter queues
AWS SQS: when you're already on AWS, no ops overhead
Kafka: when you need durable, replayable event streams at scale
patterns: Retry with exponential backoff and jitter
Dead letter queues for failed jobs

three_pillars.logs: Structured logging (JSON). Correlation IDs across service boundaries. Log levels and when to use each. Never log secrets.
three_pillars.metrics: RED method: Rate, Errors, Duration. USE method: Utilization, Saturation, Errors. Custom business metrics.
three_pillars.traces: Distributed tracing (OpenTelemetry). Trace every request through your system. Span attributes and events.

observability.tools: Datadog, Grafana + Prometheus + Loki + Tempo (open source stack), AWS CloudWatch

resources.title: Designing Data-Intensive Applications
resources.why: The bible of backend engineering. Read it twice.

resources.title: PostgreSQL Documentation
resources.why: Read the internals section. Know your database.

resources.title: Redis University
resources.why: Free, official Redis courses for real-world patterns

resources.title: Hussein Nasser Backend Engineering
resources.why: Deep dives on database internals, proxies, and backend concepts

monolith.when: Early stage, small team (< 5 engineers), unclear domain boundaries.
monolith.advantages: Simple deployment, easy debugging, no network latency between modules.
monolith.migration_path: Modular monolith first. Extract services only when you have clear, stable domain boundaries and team scalability requirements.

microservices.when: Large org with multiple teams, proven domain boundaries, need independent deployment.
microservices.cost: Distributed systems complexity, network failures, eventual consistency, operational overhead.
microservices.reality_check: Most companies that built microservices from day one regretted it. Amazon and Netflix migrated TO microservices, they didn't start there.

serverless.when: Variable/unpredictable traffic, event-driven workloads, small team that can't run Kubernetes.
serverless.tradeoffs: Cold starts, vendor lock-in, harder debugging, cost model changes at scale.

scalability_principles.horizontal_vs_vertical: Scale vertically first (simpler), horizontally when you hit limits. Design for horizontal from day one.
scalability_principles.stateless_services: Services should not hold state. State lives in databases and caches. This enables horizontal scaling.
database_scaling: Sharding as last resort—massive operational complexity
CQRS when read and write models diverge significantly
scalability_principles.load_balancing: Round-robin, least connections, IP hash. Sticky sessions break horizontal scaling—avoid.
scalability_principles.rate_limiting: Token bucket (allows bursting), sliding window (smooth), fixed window (simple but bursty). Implement at the edge.

real_time_systems.websockets: Full-duplex. Use for chat, live collaboration, gaming. Requires sticky sessions or pub/sub backend (Redis Pub/Sub).
real_time_systems.server_sent_events: One-directional server push. Simpler than WebSockets. Good for dashboards, notifications. Works with HTTP/2 multiplexing.
real_time_systems.long_polling: Fallback when WebSockets unavailable. Inefficient but compatible.
real_time_systems.webrtc: Peer-to-peer. Video/audio, file transfer, screen sharing. STUN/TURN servers for NAT traversal.

patterns: Event sourcing: state as a sequence of events, not current value
CQRS: separate read and write models
Outbox pattern: reliable event publishing with database writes

failure_handling.circuit_breaker: Prevent cascade failures. Open circuit on threshold failures, half-open to probe recovery.
failure_handling.bulkhead: Isolate failures. Separate thread pools/connection pools per downstream service.
failure_handling.retry: Exponential backoff with jitter. Idempotency keys for safe retries.
failure_handling.timeout: Every network call needs a timeout. Default to 3-5 seconds for synchronous, longer for async.
failure_handling.chaos_engineering: Netflix Chaos Monkey principle. Test failure recovery in production with controlled chaos.

resources.title: System Design Interview (Alex Xu)
resources.why: Best practical guide to system design interviews and real-world patterns

resources.title: High Scalability Blog
resources.why: Real architecture posts from companies at scale

resources.title: Martin Fowler's Blog
resources.why: Canonical patterns: CQRS, Event Sourcing, Microservices, Strangler Fig

resources.title: ByteByteGo Newsletter
resources.why: Visual system design explanations that stick

principles: Everything as code: pipelines, infrastructure, configs
Fast feedback loops: fail fast, fail early
Trunk-based development over long-lived feature branches
cicd.tools: GitHub Actions (default for most), CircleCI (speed), ArgoCD (GitOps), Tekton (Kubernetes-native)

containerization.security: Scan images (Trivy, Snyk), never hardcode secrets, use Docker secrets or env from vault

core_concepts: Ingress controllers: Nginx, Traefik, Gateway API
production_patterns: Pod Disruption Budgets for zero-downtime deploys
kubernetes.tools: Helm for package management, kustomize for env overlays, k9s for CLI management

aws.core_services: EC2, RDS, S3, CloudFront, Lambda, EKS, SQS/SNS, ElastiCache, Route53
aws.when_to_choose: Largest ecosystem, most enterprises. Best when team has existing AWS expertise or needs maximum service breadth.

gcp.core_services: GKE (best managed Kubernetes), Cloud Run (best serverless containers), BigQuery, Cloud SQL, Pub/Sub
gcp.when_to_choose: Best Kubernetes experience (GKE). Best for data/ML workloads. Strong networking.

azure.core_services: AKS, Azure Functions, CosmosDB, Azure AD, DevOps
azure.when_to_choose: Enterprise companies with Microsoft/Active Directory dependency. Strong for .NET shops.

infrastructure_as_code.terraform: Default choice. HCL is readable. Massive provider ecosystem. State management is the hard part.
infrastructure_as_code.pulumi: Code-based IaC (TypeScript/Python). Better for complex logic. Smaller community.
infrastructure_as_code.cdk: AWS Cloud Development Kit. Best for AWS-only shops. Full programming language power.
principles: State stored remotely (S3 + DynamoDB for Terraform)
Workspaces or directories for environment isolation

monitoring_alerting.philosophy: Alert on symptoms, not causes. Page on SLO violations, not CPU spikes.
monitoring_alerting.slos_slis_slas: SLI: what you measure. SLO: your target. SLA: contract with customers. Start with availability, latency, error rate.
monitoring_alerting.stack: Prometheus + Grafana (open source). Datadog (full-featured, expensive). AWS CloudWatch (good enough for AWS-native).
monitoring_alerting.alerting: PagerDuty or OpsGenie for on-call rotation. Runbooks for every alert. No alert without a clear action.

resources.title: AWS Well-Architected Framework
resources.why: Official framework for production AWS systems

resources.title: Kubernetes.io Documentation
resources.why: The authoritative source

resources.title: The DevOps Handbook
resources.why: Cultural and technical foundations of high-performing engineering orgs

resources.title: Cloud Native Patterns
resources.why: Patterns for building resilient cloud-native applications

clean_code.naming: Code is read 10x more than it's written. Names are the primary documentation. If you need a comment to explain what code does, the code is wrong.
clean_code.functions: Do one thing. Do it well. Functions that take boolean flags do two things—split them.
clean_code.abstractions: The right abstraction is the hardest part of software engineering. Wrong abstraction is worse than duplication (Rule of Three, not DRY at all costs).
anti_patterns: Functions over 50 lines that should be multiple
Comments explaining what instead of why

design_patterns.when_to_apply: Patterns are solutions to problems. If you don't have the problem, don't apply the pattern.

most_valuable.repository: Abstract data access. Enables testing. Separates business logic from persistence.
most_valuable.factory: Abstract object creation. Useful when creation logic is complex or varies.
most_valuable.observer: Event-driven communication. Decouple producers from consumers.
most_valuable.strategy: Swap algorithms at runtime. Replace conditionals with polymorphism.
most_valuable.adapter: Make incompatible interfaces compatible. Critical in legacy system integration.
most_valuable.facade: Simplify complex subsystems. Provide a clean API over messy internals.

design_patterns.over_engineering_warning: If someone junior to you can't understand why you used the pattern, you've over-engineered it.

as_author: Small PRs (< 400 lines). Large PRs don't get reviewed properly.
Context in description: what, why, how, how to test
as_reviewer: Nit comments are optional, mark them as such
Ask questions instead of making demands
Block on: security issues, correctness bugs, breaking API changes
Review the tests first—they tell you what the code should do

technical_documentation.adrs: Architecture Decision Records: context, decision, consequences. Living documentation of why things are the way they are.
technical_documentation.runbooks: Operational documentation: how to diagnose and fix production issues. Written by the person who built it.
technical_documentation.api_documentation: OpenAPI/Swagger for REST. Proto files for gRPC. Keep in sync with code—generate from code when possible.
technical_documentation.readmes: Setup, architecture overview, key decisions, links to further documentation. Maintained as part of PR checklist.

resources.why: The best book on reducing software complexity

resources.why: Visual, practical guide to design patterns and refactoring

projects.title: Work Report: Real Projects Progression
projects.subtitle: Projects that prove mastery through building, not just learning

tiers.goal: Build foundational muscle memory. Ship working products.

projects.title: Full-Stack Blog Platform
projects.problem: Build a performant content platform with auth, markdown editing, and SEO optimization.

stack.backend: Next.js API Routes or Hono, PostgreSQL (Neon or Supabase)

projects.stack_justification: Production-grade stack used at scale. Forces understanding of SSR, API design, and database basics.
skills_proven: Next.js App Router and rendering strategies
Database schema design and SQL basics
SEO: meta tags, sitemap, structured data

success_metrics.stretch: Add RSS feed, email notifications, comment system

projects.title: Real-Time Chat Application
projects.problem: Build a Slack-like messaging system with channels, DMs, and real-time updates.

stack.database: PostgreSQL for messages, Redis for presence

projects.stack_justification: Forces understanding of stateful connections, event-driven patterns, and real-time state synchronization.

success_metrics.performance: Messages delivered in < 100ms on local network

tiers.goal: Production-grade systems with proper architecture, testing, and observability.

projects.title: E-Commerce Platform with Payments
projects.problem: Build a production-ready store with inventory management, payment processing, and order fulfillment.

stack.deployment: AWS (ECS or EKS) or GCP Cloud Run

projects.stack_justification: Covers payment flows (critical skill), webhook handling, inventory consistency, and distributed system basics.
skills_proven: Feature flags with LaunchDarkly or Flipt

success_metrics.tests: > 80% code coverage, all payment flows e2e tested
success_metrics.reliability: Stripe webhook processing with retry and deduplication

projects.title: Developer Analytics Dashboard
projects.problem: Build a system that ingests GitHub/GitLab events and provides engineering team metrics.

stack.ingestion: Kafka or SQS for event streaming
stack.storage: TimescaleDB or ClickHouse for time-series metrics

projects.stack_justification: Introduces event-driven architecture, time-series data, and data visualization at the backend level.

features: PR cycle time, deployment frequency, DORA metrics
Real-time event ingestion from GitHub webhooks

tiers.goal: Distributed systems, team collaboration, and architectural decision-making.

projects.title: Multi-Tenant SaaS Platform
projects.problem: Build a complete SaaS application with team workspaces, billing, and role-based access.

stack.architecture: Modular monolith or microservices (justify your choice)
stack.infra: Kubernetes on AWS EKS or GKE

projects.stack_justification: SaaS is the dominant software model. Multi-tenancy introduces data isolation, billing complexity, and organizational hierarchy challenges.

success_metrics.architecture: Written ADRs for all major decisions
success_metrics.security: Tenant data isolation verified with automated tests
success_metrics.billing: Subscription create, upgrade, downgrade, cancel flows tested
success_metrics.ops: Zero-downtime deployment, monitoring dashboards, alerting configured

projects.title: Collaborative Document Editor
projects.problem: Build a Google Docs-like real-time collaborative editor with conflict resolution.

stack.deployment: Fly.io or Railway with WebSocket support

projects.stack_justification: CRDTs are the cutting edge of distributed systems in frontend. Forces deep understanding of operational transforms vs. CRDTs.

success_metrics.correctness: No data loss under simulated network partitions

tiers.goal: Contribute to the ecosystem. Build things used by thousands of engineers.

projects.title: Open Source Developer Tool
projects.problem: Identify a genuine pain point in your daily workflow and build the tool you wish existed.
examples: Database migration tool with better DX than Flyway
Test data factory with type safety
GraphQL schema linter with custom rules
skills_proven: API design for other developers (the hardest design problem)

success_metrics.quality: Full test suite, TypeScript types, zero known security vulnerabilities
success_metrics.documentation: README, API docs, migration guides, examples repository

projects.title: High-Scale Data Pipeline
projects.problem: Build a system that processes and analyzes millions of events per day with sub-second query latency.

stack.processing: Apache Flink or Kafka Streams for stream processing
stack.api: GraphQL over ClickHouse with smart caching

workflow.title: Daily → Weekly → Monthly Execution System
workflow.philosophy: Consistency compounds. A 1-hour daily practice beats a 10-hour weekend binge every time.

daily.title: Daily Habits of Top Developers
daily.time_budget: Assumes 1-2 hours of focused learning/practice outside work

activities: Read one technical article or paper (HackerNews, morning.news, or saved queue)
Review previous day's code with fresh eyes
Set three specific learning goals for the day
Log blockers immediately—don't spiral on problems > 20 minutes without seeking help
Log what you shipped and learned

non_negotiables: Write code every day (even 30 minutes counts)
Read others' code (open source is a free mentorship program)
Never end a day without understanding why something worked

weekly.title: Weekly Rhythm

time_allocation.building: 60% — Your primary project. Real progress on real features.
time_allocation.learning: 20% — Structured study: courses, books, documentation deep dives.
time_allocation.reading_code: 15% — Read production-grade open source code in your domain.
time_allocation.community: 5% — Engage in technical communities, write notes/blog posts.

weekly_ritual.monday: Week planning: What will you ship by Friday? Be specific.
weekly_ritual.wednesday: Mid-week check: Are you on track? What's blocking you?
weekly_ritual.friday: Week retrospective: What did you ship? What did you learn? What will you do differently?

code_reading_sources: Linear app (if you can get access—exceptional engineering blog)
tRPC source code (clean API design patterns)

monthly.title: Monthly Evaluation
evaluation_checklist: Can I explain what I built to a senior engineer without notes?
What problem did I solve that I couldn't solve 30 days ago?
Have I shipped something into production (personal or work)?
Have I read one book chapter or completed one module of a course?
Have I helped someone else (review, answer question, write documentation)?
What's my current weakest area, and do I have a plan to address it?

upgrade_criteria.stay_in_current_tier: You're still learning fundamentals of current tier projects
upgrade_criteria.move_to_next_tier: You can explain all decisions in your current tier project and identify what's missing
red_flags: You've been studying without building for > 2 weeks
You can't explain why you chose your stack
You haven't written tests for a project you care about
You'd be embarrassed to show your code to a senior engineer

developer_levels.title: Developer Levels & Growth Trajectories

levels.title: Junior Engineer
technical_markers: Implements well-defined features with guidance
Follows existing patterns in the codebase
Writes tests when asked
Needs significant review before merge
Asks 'how do I implement X?' frequently
behavioral_markers: Needs tasks broken down into < 1 day chunks
Asks for help frequently (which is correct!)
levels.growth_focus: Speed of execution and pattern recognition. Build the muscle memory.
common_blockers: Underestimating how much is in code they 'understand'

levels.title: Mid-Level Engineer
technical_markers: Implements features independently
Identifies edge cases and failure modes proactively
Writes comprehensive tests
Reviews junior PRs effectively
Starts proposing solutions, not just implementing them
levels.growth_focus: Depth in your primary domain. Start thinking about system design.
common_blockers: Mid-level plateau: good enough to ship, not growing
Collecting skills (courses) instead of using them

levels.title: Senior Engineer
technical_markers: Designs systems from scratch with clear tradeoffs
Anticipates scaling and failure modes in design reviews
Defines team coding standards
Mentors mid-level engineers
Writes ADRs and technical proposals
behavioral_markers: Makes technical tradeoffs in terms of business impact
Pushes back on PMs when timeline/quality tradeoffs are wrong
levels.growth_focus: Multiplying others. Your personal output matters less than your team's output.
levels.what_separates_exceptional: Exceptional seniors make everyone around them faster. They write the ADRs no one asked for. They fix the on-call pain no one prioritized. They grow the engineer behind them.

levels.title: Staff Engineer
technical_markers: Drives technical strategy across teams
Makes architectural decisions with 2-5 year time horizons
Identifies and resolves cross-team technical dependencies
Builds technical consensus across disagreeing groups
behavioral_markers: Manages technical risk at org level
Partners with VPs/Directors on technical strategy
levels.growth_focus: Organizational influence. Technical strategy. Writing that shapes how others think.
levels.rarity: Less than 5% of engineers reach Staff level. The bottleneck is influence, not skill.

levels.title: Principal Engineer
technical_markers: Sets technical direction for the entire company
Recognized externally (talks, papers, OSS)
Builds engineering culture through example and writing
Resolves the most critical technical challenges
behavioral_markers: Trusted to make irreversible architectural decisions
Defines what 'good engineering' means at the company
levels.growth_focus: Legacy. What do you leave behind? How do you shape the next generation?
levels.honest_note: This level often requires being at the right company at the right time, in addition to exceptional technical skill.

universal_growth_blockers: Comfort zone addiction: doing what you're already good at
Avoiding feedback by avoiding code review, talks, and writing
Depth without communication: being technically excellent but unable to explain
Breadth without depth: a portfolio of half-finished projects
Confusing seniority with years of experience (they don't correlate after year 3)
Failing to understand the business impact of technical decisions

what_separates_exceptional.technical: They know why their tools work, not just how to use them. They can reason from first principles when no prior art exists.
what_separates_exceptional.communication: They write with clarity. Their design docs are read, their PRs are approved faster, their incidents are resolved quicker.
what_separates_exceptional.judgment: They know when NOT to build, when to use off-the-shelf, and when to pay technical debt now vs. later.
what_separates_exceptional.reliability: When they say it will be done Friday, it's done Friday. When there's an incident, they're the person everyone wants on the call.
what_separates_exceptional.curiosity: They're genuinely excited by hard problems. They read papers, post-mortems, and changelogs for fun.
